content:
- FEB 07, 2023
Good morning everyone. This is the first of the price action Chronicles. We will begin with a review of our
current market position relative to past performance. Following this, I will provide my analysis for the E30
and expectations for the 9:30 AM trading session.

The session will utilize a raw chart view on TradingView, with all indicators and tools visible at the bottom to
ensure transparency in my actions and analysis. This approach allows for clear demonstration, especially
when comparing charts.

- THE RISK BAROMETER
We begin with the Dollar Index because it serves as a risk-on/risk-off barometer. It indicates whether I'm
likely to see continued upside (going long in ES) or continued weakness in ES. It also helps in anticipating
consolidation, choppiness, or conflicting analysis, which guides my participation level (e.g., small intraday
scalps instead of full session runs).

All my analysis, across Forex, bond markets, equities, and index futures, starts with the Dollar Index. It
frames the logic for whether I should assume risk (e.g., buying foreign currencies or stocks), which
correlates with a lower dollar (risk-on). At the beginning of the year, we were indeed looking for a lower
dollar, as documented in my YouTube analysis and Twitter commentary.
DXY
- TARGETING LIQUIDITY AND FAIR VALUE GAPS
Our prior analysis for DXY looked for lower prices and sell-side liquidity, which was absolutely tagged. If
price went below that, we identified an order block and a fair value gap. This fair value gap is a buy-side
imbalance sell-side inefficiency, occurring between a candle's high and a subsequent candle's low.

I emphasize that my concepts are not supply and demand. For a fair value gap (specifically classified as a
buy-side imbalance sell-side inefficiency or BISI), there are three specific price levels to watch:
- The actual high, framed by the preceding candle's low.
- The middle, which is consequent encroachment.
- The low, which is the least likely price objective.
The two primary levels when trading down into it are the high of the gap and its midpoint (consequent
encroachment). Price action clearly shows a reaction at these levels.

section_title: DXY'S REACTION AND CURRENT RISK SCENARIO
content:
We observed the DXY's reaction, an undeniable bounce off the consequent encroachment. This confirmed
my framing of shorts on the S&P last week, as DXY had traded down into this imbalance. Currently, the
market has posted a risk-off scenario, with the dollar going higher. This implies foreign currencies are likely
to go lower, and equities will likely see temporary weakness.

If you see shorting opportunities in the S&P and your charts indicate a potential low resistance liquidity run,
be prepared. However, not every price gyration is a setup I'm interested in. My purpose here is to teach you
how to read the tape before you even use a demo account. It's about building a solid foundation, not
chasing every trade. Don't let my specific setups discourage you from your own profitable models.
DXY
- BEARISH ORDER BLOCKS AND MEAN THRESHOLD
The dollar has already traded up into a bearish order block. A bearish order block is the last up-close
candle prior to a displacement lower with a fair value gap. Its midpoint is called the mean threshold. Half of
a gap is consequent encroachment, while half of an order block is mean threshold. I'm watching to see if
DXY trades through this mean threshold, as it could indicate a draw towards buy-side liquidity above
relative equal highs.
DXY
- IMMEDIATE EXPECTATIONS AND PRICE REACTIONS
If DXY continues to press higher, it suggests potential for lower prices in Forex, stocks, and stock averages.
Alternatively, we could see a complete closure of the fair value gap, leading to a sell-off towards a breaker,
around the 102.30 to 102.50 price range. We're observing a quiet overnight session, with light news
expected at 8:30 AM (ET).

- LIQUIDITY AND EFFICIENCY
The Euro drew up into relative equal highs for buy-side liquidity, as predicted earlier this year. It then traded
perfectly into a sell-side imbalance buy-side inefficiency (SIBI) fair value gap. This is the market
algorithmically rebalancing and re-pricing the range. After hitting this, the market displaced lower, taking out
a short-term swing low and trading into a buy-side imbalance sell-side efficiency (BISI) fair value gap.

Just because charts are printing doesn't mean you should take a trade. There must be a logical, high
- probability condition to lean on. I don't subscribe to traditional risk-to-reward models, as you can't
guarantee outcomes. My goal is to teach you to identify opportunities where price action has the highest
probability of moving from one level to the next, not to put you in winning trades. This is about learning to
read price before engaging in demo or live trading. I will no longer be engaging on Twitter, it is solely for
alerts.

- RELATIVE WEAKNESS AND DXY INFLUENCE
Cable (GBP/USD) showed weakness, failing to make a higher high compared to the Euro. It looks likely to
move down into sell-side liquidity. If the dollar accelerates upside (risk-off), Cable could draw down into
imbalances at lower levels. Dollar strength means lower prices for foreign currencies and stock indices.

section_title: ES CHART LAYOUT AND COMPARATIVE INDEX ANALYSIS
content:
My ES trading setup includes:
- Upper left: Hourly chart.
- Lower left: 15-minute chart.
- Right: A variable timeframe chart (1, 2, 3, 4, or 5-minute).
This mimics my physical eight-monitor setup. I primarily trade ES and NASDAQ (NQ), not the Dow (YM),
but will mention it when relevant. My focus is on naked price action, without indicators.

section_title: TRADINGVIEW COMPARISON TOOLS AND MARKET BREADTH
content:
To compare indices like NASDAQ (NQ) or Dow (YM) to ES on TradingView:
- Click the '+' symbol.
- Type the front-month code (e.g., NQH2023 for NASDAQ, YMH2023 for Dow).
- Select 'New Pane' to plot it separately.
- For comparative study, change the price source from 'Close' to 'High' or 'Low' on each comparison chart.
This allows observation of market breadth and Dow Theory to confirm if price moves are likely to continue.

- IDENTIFICATION AND PURPOSE
The New Week Opening Gap (NWOG) is a critical concept. On Sunday, as soon as the market opens, I
mark the opening price. This, combined with Friday's closing price, defines the NWOG range. This gap acts
as a very strong price magnet, especially when the market is consolidating rather than trending. Price often
gravitates back to this gap, sometimes filling halfway, sometimes completely, acting as classic support and
resistance.

To identify the midpoint of the NWOG, use a Fibonacci tool from Sunday's opening price to Friday's closing
price. This midpoint is also considered consequent encroachment. For example, the midpoint might be
4140.25. These levels (high, low, and midpoint) are highly sensitive. We haven't meaningfully probed
anything above the consequent encroachment of the NWOG, meaning there's some unfilled buy-side
liquidity above.

section_title: ES MORNING BIAS AND MARKET MANIPULATION
content:
Even if ES goes lower this morning (which is likely), a weakening dollar could present an opportunity for ES
to trade back up into the NWOG. I view markets like stock indices as 'synthetic,' lacking the fundamental
supply and demand factors of commodities. Therefore, I focus on areas where manipulation is obvious or
expected, looking for setups that repeat based on price trading to liquidity.

- LIQUIDITY & 2022 MODEL
My trading approach is about identifying liquidity. Price moves to buy-side liquidity (above a swing high or
relative equal highs) or sell-side liquidity (below a swing low or relative equal lows). These liquidity points
can serve as entries for new trades or targets for existing ones. The 2022 model emphasizes: first,
observing buy-side liquidity being taken, and then looking for a displacement lower, breaking a short-term
swing low. This indicates a bearish market structure shift.

- ENTRY RULES AND PSYCHOLOGICAL PITFALLS
After a market structure shift (e.g., displacement lower after taking buy-side liquidity), identify the price leg
responsible for the displacement. Look for an inefficiency or fair value gap within this leg, above equilibrium
(the 50% level on a Fibonacci of the leg). This FVG is your entry point. For shorts, you enter during 'up
candles,' which can feel scary initially. Overcoming the desire to be 'right' and embracing uncertainty, while
focusing on profitability and risk management, is crucial. One losing trade does not define your career.

To refine entry, identify the specific price leg (from high to the low that triggered the market shift). The price
above the 50% level (equilibrium) of this leg is the 'premium market' (where you want to sell/short). Price
below 50% is 'discount.' The highest probability shorts are in the premium market. Once price crosses the
midpoint of the discount level, high-probability pyramiding entries are exhausted. At that point, the focus
shifts to taking partials and trailing stops, not adding new positions.

- VOLUME IMBALANCE AND ORDER BLOCKS
When selecting a fair value gap for entry:
- Look for a bearish order block (last up-close candle before displacement with an FVG).
- Consider a volume imbalance (VI)
- a separation between a candle's close and another's open, with only
wicks overlapping. This is a 'glitch in the matrix.'
The mean threshold (midpoint) of a bearish order block serves as a precise stop-loss level. For example, if
a bearish order block's mean threshold is 4131.50, your stop could be at 4132.00, resulting in a small 2
- point stop loss.

section_title: REFINED FVG SELECTION AND JOURNALING IMPORTANCE
content:
To pick the right fair value gap, combine it with a volume imbalance and a bearish order block. This gives
you algorithmic precision for entry. You must write this down, record it in your vlog, and journal it; don't just
passively watch. If no volume imbalance or specific order block FVG is present, use the first fair value gap
above the equilibrium of the displacement leg.

- THE GAME OF TAG AND POSITION MANAGEMENT
Volume imbalances are 'glitches in the matrix' or coded signatures for price to react. Smart money operates
a 'game of tag': they sell short into buy stops (liquidity above highs), anticipating lower prices. Then, they
buy to cover their shorts into sell stops (liquidity below lows). This turns traditional support/resistance
upside down. Once price enters the 'discount' range (below the midpoint of the displacement leg), it's no
longer an ideal area for new high-probability entries; it's time to manage the existing position and take
partials.

The ES delivered precisely to the predicted sell-side liquidity, demonstrating the 2022 model. This
morning's move from 4118 to 4113 was a 5-handle run. For new traders, consistently achieving a 5-handle
move on paper trading is a crucial starting point. This objective is frequent and manageable, allowing you
to build confidence and leverage the power of compound interest through disciplined money management.
Always revisit macro analysis (like DXY) before focusing on lower timeframe charts.

section_title: RISK-ON/RISK-OFF EXPLAINED AND CONSISTENCY IN TRADING
content:
To clarify the risk-on/risk-off dynamic:
- Dollar lower (risk-on) -> S&P, Index Futures, and Forex likely to go higher.
- Dollar higher (risk-off) -> S&P, Index Futures, and Forex likely to go lower.
We briefly reviewed Euro (softer, but tradable), Cable (weak, stopped out in an example), and Gold (drop
due to DXY strength). It is vital to maintain a consistent approach and give yourself a chance to do well,
rather than jumping between methods or markets after a losing trade.

- CAUTION, GAPS, AND LIQUIDITY TRAPS
At the ES open, we observe a fair value gap. However, it's not a tradable entry because price has already
moved significantly and tapped into sell-side liquidity. We must be cautious. The market might sell off
initially to entice short-sellers ('street money'), then reverse and rip higher (a 'fake-out'). It's also important
to consider gaps between Electronic Trading Hours (ETH) and Regular Trading Hours (RTH). When a
significant gap exists, the market is more likely to trade back up into that range rather than continue lower
immediately.
